chunk_summary: Structured outpatient history and feeding observation for wasting focuses on assessing appetite, feeding function, illness drivers, and complications, alongside real-time feeding observation to guide management plans.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical assessment of moderate wasting beyond anthropometric measures.
Identify illness drivers and contextual constraints contributing to wasting.
Perform feeding observations to detect unsafe swallowing and caregiver-child interaction issues.
Recognize early complications and micronutrient deficiencies affecting follow-up intensity.
key_facts: Moderate wasting should be assessed as a clinical syndrome, not just an anthropometric label.
Key disposition drivers include appetite, recent weight loss or falling MUAC, and danger signs (ETAT/IMNCI).
Feeding observation detects unsafe swallowing, inappropriate textures, and poor caregiver-child interaction.
Examination should prioritize hydration status, respiratory rate, pallor, and bilateral pitting oedema.
Micronutrient deficiencies like periorificial dermatitis, angular cheilitis, and Bitot spots should be documented.
Abdominal distension or hepatosplenomegaly may indicate chronic infection, TB, or malabsorption.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Moderate wasting should be assessed as a clinical syndrome, not only an anthropometry label. (Reference 1)
